items.why: Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: What tense follows 'depuis' for ongoing situations in French?
items.correct: Present tense (J'habite ici DEPUIS 5 ans - I have lived here for 5 years).
items.misconception: Past tense (because it is translated as 'have lived' in English).
items.why: Students translate the English tense literally. French uses PRESENT with depuis because the action is ongoing - 'I am still living'. The English perfect tense misleads students into using past in French.

items.q: In which year did Algeria gain independence from France?
items.why: It's 1962. “1954” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is NOT part of the Maghreb?
items.why: It's Sénégal. “Algérie” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Quebec's Law 101 (Charter of the French Language) passed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1977. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
